sentences.text: Yes.
I don't know what-- what you mean by family.
Immediate family or--
Yes, yes, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
No.
I am not sure that there were names to the streets in the neighborhood we lived.
Yes.
I-- I don't recall that I missed anything, from toys to-- to any-- anything.
And I don't recall that-- that anybody we visited missed anything.
Yes.
Well, she was my mother.
It was--
No, I-- I-- with her I remember mostly staying in.
In the house, and playing in the garden.
She would-- she would-- I had a-- I had a car, one of those cars that you push with pedals.
And she-- she would put a string on it.
I think I may even have a picture.
I have very few pictures from that time.
Many pictures which were taken by my aunt and transferred to me by my cousin after she died.
But I-- I recall that I-- I was too short to push the pedals.
With the motorcar, I always remember, they always bought things which are a bit bigger.
You know, the-- the clothes were a bit bigger.
So they will stay.
And they would kind of fold the sweater.
And the car was, so I can have it for a longer time.
I couldn't get to the pedals easily.
So they-- she put a string [INAUDIBLE] on-- on the car and pulled me around the garden and where she went shopping.
My recollection is that it was a large house.
But again, I'm very careful in reflecting on sizes from the point of view of a three-year-old child, four-year-old child.
Essentially till we fled in 1944, summer of 1944.
I don't know, from my own knowledge, obviously at the time.
All I know is that one-- one point, a strange man came to the house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't recognize him at first.
And then-- then I realized it was my father.
He was so very strange because as a rabbi, and the Jew, he had a long beard, a long, flowing brown beard.
And he shaved it off, beard and mustache and all.
I didn't recognize him obviously [? for me. ?] It was a different man [INAUDIBLE].
My mother told me that he had to shave it, and he has to go.
And we have to go.
Yes.
And he disappeared, not to be seen by me for many months.
And my mother took me, and my sister, and some gatherings.
I remember that I carried something.
I don't remember what.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were dressed warmly, with coats and thick socks and shoes and hats.
And we are put in an ambulance.
It was the end of the-- it was the end of the summer.
We were put in an ambulance and were taken-- closed ambulance, not-- not one of those-- [?
I mean, ?] all the windows, everything was closed.
And we were sitting back where the sick are, not-- not next to the driver.
And we were just told to-- to sit and be quiet.
And we left.
The ambulance broke at one point in time.
And they hitched some horses to it.
And we went on and on and eventually abandoned the ambulance.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't recall how many days or weeks we were on the road.
All I know that we wandered through mountains.
It was very nice for me.
I liked it a lot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- we ate berries and drank from the-- from the very clear-- it was not rivers-- little springs that are so abundant in the mountains.
Slovakia is a nice country, if they don't kill you all the time.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we-- we stay in huts.
There are huts in the mountains for hunters and for skiers and for shepherds.
It was a totally agricultural community in that area.
So there were huts for the shepherds.
And-- and we had milk and cheese from the shepherds.
And I don't recall how long we were on the road.
But I recall it with great pleasure.
I'm sure my mother didn't think it was a great pleasure.
Just the three of us.
No, But clearly somebody sympathetic and clearly somebody Christian.
I know that we stopped a couple of times.
Nobody opened the ambulance.
I don't know if we stopped for roadblocks or we stopped for-- just stopped.
We're not the only one on the road.
Eventually, we abandoned it and went from one place to another, and never to stay long.
The only place I remember staying long was Banska Bystrica.
No, not at all.
I knew about the Tatra Mountains, which is not far away from there.
Banska Bystrica was a name known to me, but not a city known to me because it was a big city.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So we-- we arrived in that place.
And that-- that the first place that I remember that I stayed for a long time, the three of us, in an apartment in a-- in a place more organized than-- than just a hut.
It was not in the mountain.
And I remember walking in Banska Bystrica.
It was the-- it was under the non-German rule, I mean-- not-- not the Slovak rule.
It was the uprising.

And that was the only-- that was really the only place that I can describe to you, between Nove Mesto, except this beautiful recollection of going to the mountain and beautiful, possibly cold weather.
But climbing up, climbing down, it was a big outing for me.
I didn't realize what was happening.
And I have to credit my mother with it.
There was no sense of panic that I recall from that time at all.
It was just, we have to go.
And we go.
She was very young.
And she was carried most of the time.
Nothing.
I knew nothing about the uprising then at all.
I became very much interested in-- in what's happening only later in my life.
And I read very much about the uprising.
It's-- it's-- I think, an intriguing story.
And it reminds me very much of-- of movies like Rashomon, where three people have four different views, so to speak, of what happens.

My view is formed or formulated by couple of approaches, being born in Slovakia and living through that period, being Jewish, and then being, later on, a member of the Israeli armed forces and the Ministry of Defense, and very much interested in-- in tactics and military history.
I was always interested in military history, since I became in high school, really, since I became aware of intellectual endeavors.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

So from all these points of view, which are very different from my point of view of a Slovak living in Slovakia, the uprising was instigated by Communist forces with cooperation, however, with Western forces.
There was a tension between them.
From the military point of view, it was the most foolish thing which can happen.
If you look at the map, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the area of the uprising would sever the lines of retreat of the Germans from southern Europe, back through Poland to Germany.
We couldn't allow such a base for allies.
It was clear that if something like that happened, it will be-- have to be crushed militarily.
Having said that-- just look at the map.
Having said that, the Russian army was just across the border.
The Russian army would want it.
They could have intervened.
And the slaughter could have stopped.
I don't know what the political aims of the Russians were.
But I don't think the Russians were averse to the idea of weakening the infrastructure of independent politics in the areas they wanted to take control over.
And I think they callously let the Germans slaughter the Slovaks.
And the-- and their own forces, which were sent, and the Western forces, and let the Slovaks slaughter some of the Germans.
They showed it, in the future, that the Russians are ready to fight to the last Bulgarian, so to speak.
And I think that was politically a callous decision by the Russians.
But that's my interpretation.
The bottom line is that militarily, the German had to crush it because it was endangering the main lines of communication with their other forces.
They invaded Slovakia, took over.
And they crushed the rebellion.
Through the air.
And from-- not only from the Russians, also from the Western powers.
There was an air-- airfield in Tri Duby-- that's the name of the place.
And there were regular flights there.
There were also drops.
There's also, you know, smuggling through the mountains from the Russian side.
They typically killed them.
Yes.
They were Jewish members of the resistance.
One of them really saved my life, when we come to that point.
They were clearly Jews walking in groups.
And they were obviously protecting themselves.
But there were signs of slaughter by the other partisans of-- of little enclaves of Jewish refugees, discovered by these Jewish partisans and documented.
Nobody like the Jews.
No, not that I know of.
The uprising was not the regular Slovak Army.
Yes.
No, the Slovaks were Judenfrei.
Oh the resistance had Jews, yes.
The resistance had Jews, both working as groups and as individuals.
Well, they-- eventually the headquarters was in Banska Bystrica because they shrunk, shrunk, shrunk, and that was the last big stronghold of the resistance, of the uprising.
And that whole area is full of monuments.
We took some pictures of some of the monuments.
The national uprising became a source of pride for the Slovaks.
And they glorify it above and beyond, in my mind, what happened.
Yes, absolutely.
Absolutely.
That was the only safe area when the German invaded.
It was the only safe area.
And I hadn't seen Germans until the time as-- as a fighting force.
So how long did you stay in Banska?
We stayed in Banska Bystrica for a while.
I cannot tell you for how long.
There is-- there are memoirs of the guy who eventually saved my life, which relate to the time.
I have copied-- let-- let me-- let me go ahead a bit in time.
It will be a bit confusing chronologically.
But I think it's logically-- it's here the place to mention it.
This man, his name is Henry Herzog, or Adam Herzog.
Illustrious history, I eventually met him.
And he had a 1,000-page document that he has written without any paragraph, almost without any periods, not divided into anything.
It was 1,000 page paragraph.
It was not a memoir.
It was-- I-- I classify it as a cry of wounded animal.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I undertook to put it in a computer.
So it's recorded.
And eventually, it was published.
I excerpted from that a little part relating to his meeting with my mother.
And I have it.
He essentially heard-- he was in Banska Bystrica.
Eventually came with all the people who came and joined the fighting group in Banska Bystrica.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And he was a-- he was a Polish Jew.
And eventually, he heard about my mother because my father was quite well-known around the jury.
And he heard that my mother was there.
And somehow they established contact And talked.
When it was clear that Banska Bystrica is going to fall to the Germans, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the party's own forces were retreating.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother pleaded with him to take her and her two children with them.
And he refused because he said they're going to the mountain.
It's no place for a woman and two little children.
In 1944, I was seven years old.
It was October.
I was just turned seven.
My sister was four years younger, three-- three or four years younger.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Eventually, she prevailed.
Eventually, she prevailed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he took her and myself and my sister with her-- with the whole partisan group, with a whole slew of refugees.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I identified on the map all the areas that we were because three years ago I went back to Slovakia eventually.
We went up a valley road.
He took us.
And we joined the partisan group, so to speak.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I-- this is something I recall very well.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Let me say something about memories.
I have very bad memory, typically.
I don't remember things except technical things.
I remember details of-- of technical devices to the iota.
But I have very bad memory generally speaking.
I don't remember faces.
I don't remember people.
I don't remember names.
I don't remember places.
The time from Banska Bystrica until-- till later, after the war, was always very vivid in my mind, to crystal detail of-- of places and-- and the way a tree would look.
And I always wondered if this is my fantasy or if this is truth.
And there was no way for me to decide.
Eventually I went back to Slovakia, to court my-- my wife, to chase away the demons, so to speak.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And what happened is that everything that I recollected was exactly the same I recollected it.
Details of stones and trees, different now in some sense, not different in others.
I'll come back to it.

Houses and-- and roads and locations and appearances and everything which I remembered was absolutely 100% correct, to the grouting in the house, the way the house was constructed, exactly where the windows were.
If you would interview me three years ago, I would tell you this is my memory.
I don't know how true it is.
But now I know it's true because this is the way I verified it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I-- I remember-- going back now chronologically-- going up that valley road, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] typical valley road of a back country in Europe at the time.
Slovakia was never a developed country among the industrialized countries in-- in Europe.
It was in the bottom of the valley, not on the side of the valley.
And there was a little river, flow more than a river, very little river, and little-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] little bridges above the river.
The road was very narrow, one lane road, really.
And it was packed.
It was totally, absolutely packed with refugees and cars.
And the cars really-- cars and horse-- horse-drawn carriages, and-- which were standard in Europe at the time.
A motorized taxi, when I was in Nove Mesto, was unusual.
We took a motorized taxi to go to Vyhne, close to Sered, to places.
But if we went to visit somebody, we went with a co-chair, with a horse-drawn carriage.
That was standard in the '40s in Europe.
In the middle of this retreat, Germans attacked.
And there was essentially a massacre in a place called Stare Hory.
Typical name, stare means old.
Hory means mountains, old mountains.
It's a place I did find on the map.
There is no monument there.
I looked for a monument.
There are monuments around.
But there is no monument to the massacre at Stare Hory.
It's very well-documented in this-- in this other book by-- by Henry.

When I read, by the way, the details of the massacre in the book, which coincided with my recollection-- when I accepted it, I-- I called it He-- eventually when he published the book, he selected that name for the book.
I can show you the book here.
I wrote a very, very short half-page introduction.
I just couldn't-- couldn't do more.
I didn't read that book either.
I had it entered into the computer.
But I couldn't do anything more.
And I did lots of editing.
But I couldn't read it.
Anyway, the Germans attacked.
And many, many people were killed.
It was an aerial attack.
And-- and that-- how-- how your life intertwines.
Later when I was in the Ministry of Defense in Israel, I was in the armament development authority.
I was always fascinated by weaponry since the Second World War-- I'll tell you a bit more about it.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was wounded.
My-- my-- my mother was killed.
My sister was killed.
And I was wounded.
And the pattern of the wound is a clear machine gun pattern that I discovered later.
At the time, I was just wounded.
I didn't even know I was wounded.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That massacre, Henry was spared.
Somebody next to him was killed, split second, half a meter behind it.
If he would stay there, he would be killed.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He took me away from there, into the mountains.
Now, oddly enough, I remember everything until that point.
I remember standing next to my mother and-- and crying and seeing my sister.
I didn't realize that they were dead.
They were just lying there.
There was no blood that I can recall.
We ran away from the-- when-- when the attack started, we ran away from the valley road, to the side of the mountain.
There was a clearing there, square clearing.
And the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the woods, the forest, started at the edge of the clearing.
I don't think it was artificial clearing.
It was just where no-- no-- no forest there.
It was-- it was not a-- it was a natural forest.
It was not a made forest.
There was a very big tree at the edge of the clearing, at the corner of the clearing, peculiar looking tree, outstanding from the other trees.
And we-- we ran toward the tree.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were-- she was killed next to the tree with my sister in the clearing.
Maybe if we could get into the woods, she would not be killed.
Everything is split second.
I was wounded.
I was-- I was there.
And I was standing.
And I was-- I was-- I was crying.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before that, I remember we were not walking on the-- on the-- we were walking on the side of the road, the side of this flows, this little river, the flow.
There was a bridge, a stone bridge, not wooden bridge, not metal, stone-- stone bridge [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on-- above this little flow.
So I remember that place there where it happened.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Apparently, he took me up the mountains with him.
He didn't know I was wounded.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was wounded in my left leg above the knee.
I was wearing what-- what we may call today woolen tights.
Children wear them-- wore them, very high woolen socks, with things which, kind of garters which keep them.
Boys, girls-- boys didn't walk with long pants, only adults.
Children had short pants even-- even in winter.
But we had these woolen thick socks.
